title: The C Vector: Preferences as Log-Probabilities

runtime.expected_outcome: Changing the log-preference on one observation bit flips the preferred action. C = softmax(logits); logits encode preference.

symbols.p(o): softmax of C; the prior over outcomes
symbols.pragmatic term: how much the agent wants obs o

audiences.kid: You write a list of what you like. Bigger numbers mean 'I want this more'. The agent picks plans that lead to the high-number things.
audiences.real: C is a vector of log-preferences over observations. The pragmatic term of G is negative expected log-preference, so bigger C entries pull action toward those observations.
audiences.equation: logits[o] = preference; C = softmax(logits); pragmatic(pi) = -sum_o q(o|pi) log p(o). Higher logits[o] -> stronger pull.
audiences.derivation: From the generative model p(o, s), factor p(o) is the prior over outcomes; log p(o) is the surprise-minimisation target. Preferences are literally log-probabilities in the agent's model (Friston et al. 2017).

orchestrate.objective: Show directly that editing C reshapes the agent's behaviour.
orchestrate.context: tiny_open_goal; custom c_preference_override swaps which observation is most preferred.

credits: Parr, Pezzulo & Friston (2022), Active Inference, MIT Press (CC BY-NC-ND), Ch 4.
Friston, FitzGerald, Rigoli, Schwartenbeck & Pezzulo (2017), Active inference: a process theory.
